---
title: "Area, Perimeter, Volume"
subtitle: "Measuring shapes — distance around, surface inside, space within"
lesson-id: "t1.area-perimeter-volume"
tier: 1
prerequisites: ["t1.shapes-angles", "t1.multiply-as-area"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area](../05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)
   and [Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](../12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)?
2. Can you compute $7 \times 8 = 56$ in your head?
3. Have you ever measured a room with a tape measure, or estimated how
   much paint you'd need for a wall?
:::

## Touch

Drag width and height. Three numbers update together: the **distance
around** the rectangle, the **surface inside**, and the **box** you'd
get if you extruded it to depth 1.

```{ojs}
viewof rW = Inputs.range([1, 10], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "Width:"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof rH = Inputs.range([1, 8], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "Height:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 720, H = 280, pad = 30;
  const cellSize = 30, gap = 1;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px;");

  // Draw rectangle as cells
  const ox = 60, oy = 30;
  for (let r = 0; r < rH; r++) {
    for (let c = 0; c < rW; c++) {
      svg.append("rect")
        .attr("x", ox + c * (cellSize + gap)).attr("y", oy + r * (cellSize + gap))
        .attr("width", cellSize).attr("height", cellSize)
        .attr("fill", "#90caf9").attr("stroke", "#2c5f7c");
    }
  }
  // Highlight perimeter cells with thicker outline
  const rectX = ox, rectY = oy;
  const rectW = rW * (cellSize + gap) - gap, rectH = rH * (cellSize + gap) - gap;
  svg.append("rect")
    .attr("x", rectX - 2).attr("y", rectY - 2)
    .attr("width", rectW + 4).attr("height", rectH + 4)
    .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#c2185b").attr("stroke-width", 3);

  // Right side text
  const tx = ox + rectW + 50;
  const perim = 2 * (rW + rH);
  const area = rW * rH;
  const vol = rW * rH * 1;
  svg.append("text").attr("x", tx).attr("y", 50).attr("font-size", "16").attr("fill", "#c2185b").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(`Perimeter (around)`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", tx).attr("y", 72).attr("font-size", "20").attr("font-family", "monospace")
    .text(`2 × (${rW} + ${rH}) = ${perim}`);

  svg.append("text").attr("x", tx).attr("y", 110).attr("font-size", "16").attr("fill", "#2c5f7c").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(`Area (inside)`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", tx).attr("y", 132).attr("font-size", "20").attr("font-family", "monospace")
    .text(`${rW} × ${rH} = ${area} sq.units`);

  svg.append("text").attr("x", tx).attr("y", 170).attr("font-size", "16").attr("fill", "#2e7d32").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(`Volume (extruded depth 1)`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", tx).attr("y", 192).attr("font-size", "20").attr("font-family", "monospace")
    .text(`${rW} × ${rH} × 1 = ${vol} cu.units`);

  return svg.node();
}
```

Three measurements, three units. **Perimeter** is a distance (1D —
metres). **Area** is a surface (2D — square metres). **Volume** is a
space (3D — cubic metres). Don't confuse them.

## Wonder

1. If you double the width of a rectangle, what happens to its
   perimeter? To its area?
2. Can two rectangles have the *same area* but *different perimeters*?
3. Why do we call square metres "square" — what is being squared?

## Discover

### Problem 1

A rectangle has width 8 m and height 5 m. Find its perimeter and its
area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Perimeter = sum of all four sides = $8 + 5 + 8 + 5$ or $2(8 + 5)$.
Area = width × height (Lesson 5).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Perimeter $= 2 \times (8 + 5) = 26$ metres.
Area $= 8 \times 5 = 40$ square metres.

Notice the **units**: perimeter is a length (metres). Area is a square
length (square metres). Same shape, two different *kinds* of measurement.
</details>

### Problem 2

A square has side **6 cm**. What is its perimeter? Its area?

Generalise: for a square of side $s$, what is the perimeter and the area
in terms of $s$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A square is a special rectangle with width = height = $s$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For $s = 6$: perimeter $= 4 \times 6 = 24$ cm; area $= 6 \times 6 = 36$
cm².

In general:
$$P = 4s, \quad A = s^2.$$

The notation $s^2$ ("$s$ squared") *is* "side × side". This is where the
phrase "**six squared**" comes from — the area of a 6-by-6 square. The
fundamental shape is hiding in the language.
</details>

### Problem 3

Sketch a rectangle with **area 12** square units. Now sketch *another*
rectangle with the same area. Are they the same shape?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
12 = 1×12 = 2×6 = 3×4. Each pairing gives a different rectangle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Three valid options: **1×12**, **2×6**, **3×4** (and their rotations:
12×1, etc., which are the same rectangles turned).

Their perimeters: $2(1+12)=26$, $2(2+6)=16$, $2(3+4)=14$. The 3×4 has
the *smallest* perimeter for area 12 — closest to a square. Among
all rectangles with a given area, **the square has the smallest
perimeter**. This is a classic *isoperimetric* problem in mathematics.

For a fixed perimeter (e.g., a rope of fixed length), the *largest area*
you can enclose with a rectangle is also a square. (And among *all*
shapes — not just rectangles — the largest area for a given perimeter
is a circle. This is one of mathematics' great theorems.)
</details>

### Problem 4

A box (rectangular cuboid) has dimensions **4 m × 3 m × 2 m**. What is
its volume?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume = length × width × height. Unit: cubic metres.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = 4 \times 3 \times 2 = 24$ m³.

Picture: stack 24 unit cubes in a 4 × 3 × 2 arrangement. The product
counts them.

Generalisation: for any rectangular cuboid, $V = \ell \times w \times h$.
For a *cube* with side $s$: $V = s^3$ ("$s$ cubed") — the same naming
trick as "squared" but in 3D.
</details>

### Problem 5

The area of a circle is given by the formula
$$A = \pi r^2,$$
where $r$ is the **radius** (distance from the centre to the edge) and
$\pi \approx 3.14159$.

A circular pizza has radius **15 cm**. What is its area?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Substitute $r = 15$. Use $\pi \approx 3.14$ for an estimate.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A = 3.14 \times 15^2 = 3.14 \times 225 \approx 706.5$ cm².

The number $\pi$ is the ratio of any circle's circumference to its
diameter — a fundamental constant of geometry. It is *irrational*,
meaning it can't be written as a fraction (Lesson 7), and its decimal
expansion (Lesson 8) goes on forever without repeating:
$\pi = 3.14159265\ldots$

We will derive these formulas properly in Tier 3 (using calculus in Tier
4, even more so). For now, accept them as given — the pizza area
calculation works the same.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Perimeter** is the total *distance around* the boundary of a 2D shape,
measured in linear units (m, cm, km).

**Area** is the *surface inside* a 2D shape, measured in square units
(m², cm²).

**Volume** is the *space inside* a 3D shape, measured in cubic units
(m³, cm³).

Key formulas (Tier 1 set):

| Shape | Perimeter | Area | (Volume, if 3D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangle (w × h) | $2(w + h)$ | $w \times h$ | — |
| Square (side $s$) | $4s$ | $s^2$ | — |
| Circle (radius $r$) | $2\pi r$ (circumference) | $\pi r^2$ | — |
| Triangle (base $b$, height $h$) | sum of 3 sides | $\tfrac{1}{2}\,b\,h$ | — |
| Cuboid ($\ell \times w \times h$) | — | (surface area, later) | $\ell \times w \times h$ |
| Cube (side $s$) | — | — | $s^3$ |

:::

> *Around the edge — perimeter, in length;*
> *Inside the edge — area, in length squared;*
> *Inside the box — volume, in length cubed.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A rectangular swimming pool is **25 m long, 10 m wide, and 2 m deep**.
> A tile costs Rs. 50 per square metre to cover the floor. What is the
> total tile cost? How much water (in cubic metres) does the pool hold?

A competent solver attacks each measurement separately:

1. **Floor area:** $25 \times 10 = 250$ m².
2. **Tile cost:** $250 \times 50 = 12{,}500$ rupees.
3. **Volume of water:** $25 \times 10 \times 2 = 500$ m³.

**Tile cost: Rs. 12,500. Water: 500 m³.** Notice each calculation uses a
different multiplication: 2D for floor, 3D for water.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A rectangle is 7 m by 4 m. Perimeter? Area?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P = 2(7+4) = 22$ m. $A = 7 \times 4 = 28$ m².
</details>

**W2.** A square has side 9 cm. Perimeter? Area?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P = 36$ cm. $A = 81$ cm².
</details>

**W3.** A box is 5 × 4 × 3 m. Volume?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = 60$ m³.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A rectangular field is 80 m by 50 m. Find the cost of fencing
it at Rs. 25 per metre.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Fencing follows the perimeter, not the area.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P = 2(80+50) = 260$ m. Cost $= 260 \times 25 = 6{,}500$ rupees.
</details>

**P2.** A circular garden has radius 7 m. Find the area covered. (Use
$\pi = \tfrac{22}{7}$ for an exact-looking answer.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A = \tfrac{22}{7} \times 7^2 = \tfrac{22}{7} \times 49 = 22 \times 7 =
154$ m². The choice of $\tfrac{22}{7}$ as the approximation makes
arithmetic clean when the radius is a multiple of 7.
</details>

**P3.** A triangular sail has base 4 m and height 3 m. What is its area?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Triangle area = $\tfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 3 = 6$ m².

The factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ has a beautiful explanation: any triangle is
exactly *half* of the rectangle that would enclose it (with the same
base and height). So a triangle's area is half of base × height. We will
prove this carefully in Tier 3.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Two cubes: one with side 2 m, the other with side 4 m. The
second cube has a side **twice** that of the first. By how much does
the *volume* multiply?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute both volumes.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
First cube: $V = 2^3 = 8$ m³. Second cube: $V = 4^3 = 64$ m³. The volume
multiplies by **8** — that is, $2^3$.

**General rule:** if you scale every dimension of a shape by a factor
$k$, then perimeter scales by $k$, area by $k^2$, and volume by $k^3$.

This is why a baby elephant is short and stout, while an adult elephant
needs proportionally thicker legs: doubling the elephant's height makes
it 8× heavier (volume), but only 4× stronger (cross-sectional area of
muscle and bone, which scales like area). At some scale, the legs can no
longer hold up the body — the **square-cube law** sets a ceiling on how
big land animals can grow. Galileo first wrote about this in 1638.
</details>

**S2.** A 3D model in a video game is a *mesh* of many tiny triangles. A
single character might have **20,000 triangles**. The graphics card
computes the area of each (for lighting and rendering). What does this
say about the importance of the triangle area formula in real-time
graphics?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Every frame of a game (60 frames per second on most modern hardware) has
to compute area-related quantities for *millions* of triangles. The
simple $\tfrac{1}{2} b h$ formula is run as a vector cross-product on a
GPU, but the underlying idea is exactly what you used in *Practice P3*.

The area, perimeter, and volume formulas you've just met are the
*foundational primitives* of computer graphics. Tier 4 (vectors) and
Tier 6 (computational geometry) build the full toolkit; Tier 1 sets the
language.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Area and perimeter are the same kind of measurement."** No —
   perimeter is a length (1D, m), area is a square length (2D, m²).
   They have different *units* and different *behaviours under scaling*
   (Stretch S1).
2. **"Volume = side × side."** That's the area of a square's face. Volume
   needs *three* multiplications, one per dimension.
3. **"Doubling the dimensions doubles the area."** No — it *quadruples*
   the area, and *octuples* the volume. The square-cube law (Stretch S1)
   surprises everyone the first time.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain to a friend why a 3×4 rectangle and a 2×6 rectangle
   have the *same area* but *different perimeters*?
2. Without scrolling back, can you compute the volume of a 5 × 6 × 2
   box?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer to #2: $V = 60$ cubic units.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[Lesson 14 — Data and the Average](../14-data-and-average/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (the square-cube law) — that result is genuinely
  surprising and worth feeling.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3* and the worked example.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](../12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)** —
  we needed names for shapes (rectangle, square, triangle, circle)
  before measuring them.
- **[Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area](../05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)** —
  the rectangle picture is the basis for both area and (extended) volume.

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 2 — Pythagoras' theorem** — a beautiful relationship between
  the *areas* of squares built on the sides of a right-angled triangle.
- **Tier 3 — Trigonometry & coordinate geometry** — once positions are
  numbers, areas become integrals.
- **Tier 4 — Calculus** — area under any curve, not just polygons.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development & graphics.** *Stretch S2* makes the case: every
rendered triangle area is computed from this formula. Volumes appear in
collision detection ("does this cube intersect that one?"), particle
systems, and physics. The Tier 1 measurements are the alphabet.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Engineering & architecture.** Floor space, paint coverage, water
tank capacity, beam strength (cross-section), heat loss (surface area).
Every building decision is some combination of perimeter, area, and
volume measurements.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** The *isoperimetric problem* (Discover P3) —
"what shape encloses the most area for a given perimeter?" — is the
ancestor of the **calculus of variations**, a major branch of analysis.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Carpet purchasing, garden planning, packaging
design, fuel-tank capacity, room ventilation, agriculture (area
yields), real estate (price per square metre).
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the cell-grid widget makes area
  literally countable), Boole (the *can two rectangles have the same
  area but different perimeters?* question is a classic Boolean
  question), Russian school (Discover P3 derives the isoperimetric
  intuition from concrete cases), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse;
  Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī* contains many area problems set in vivid
  scenes — moats, ponds, gardens), 3Blue1Brown (the square-cube law
  in S1 is exactly the kind of dimension-shifting moment 3B1B excels
  at).
- **Self-study guarantees** all present.
:::
